segs.utf8:
Transcriber: Haosen Wang
Reviewer: Le Thi Cam Van CTU

At 17 years old, I arrived in America
dreaming of starting a business.
And it was hard. Really hard.

Starting a business took time,
money and technical expertise.
And it's always been this way.
Until recently, advancements

in AI have made it easier than ever
to start a business.

With the click of a button, you can create
anything instantly and perfectly.

And yet, businesses are still
failing in record numbers.

In the first year, we lose two
out of every ten new businesses.

After five years,
half are gone, and after a decade,

only three of them are left,with all the 
new tools now at our fingertips.

Why is it still so hard
to succeed in business?
In 2012, I started my first business

with no experience distributing
water filtration products
in the industrial sector,

and I did everything I could to get
that business off the ground.

And I don't mean the hustle
culture version you see on Instagram.

I wasn't waking up at 5 a.m.
and jumping into an ice bath
or chugging bulletproof coffee.
The reality was way less exciting.

I had to cold call for hours,
send hundreds of emails,
travel constantly to trade shows,

and try to build relationships with people
who didn't even want to speak to me.
It was brutal.

But eventually I got some clients and
started making some money consistently.
And once I felt I knew what I was doing,

I used technology to build systems
and automations and the business took off.

Like many entrepreneurs,
I had many ideas so that.

So now that my first
business was a success,
I felt that I could give them a go.

But this time, I decided to use technology
right from the beginning to help me

in very little time. I had a website,
a team of remote programmers,
and it looked like I had a real business.

But as soon as I started speaking
to giant companies,

I realized that my solution
wouldn't work for them.

And for a while, I kept rolling
that boulder up a hill until I finally had

to realize that the business
was going nowhere.

I was devastated. So why
couldn't I make it work this time?
Throughout my entrepreneurial journey,

I've connected with many
aspiring business owners.

In the early days,
I used to hear things like,
I haven't got the perfect idea yet

or I don't have the money
to get started or I've got a job.

How am I supposed to find the time
for a new business?

And many would just say that the reason
they hadn't gotten started yet was

because they just didn’t know
 how to build a website
or start an email list.

But lately, the conversations
have changed.

Recently, a new entrepreneur came up
to me and she said, hey, check it out.
I used AI to build my app.

What do you think?
I said, looks very fancy.
Um, who are you going to be?
Your customers?
She hesitated and said, hmm.
I haven't quite figured that out yet.

She had an idea used AI
to create it in just a few days.
But when she launched crickets.

And this is happening
to many new businesses,

and there are a lot
of them since the pandemic,
when many of us were locked at home.
The number of new businesses skyrocketed.

But even though millions
of people have gotten started

and the overall numbers for business
formations actually doubled,
the success rate has stayed the same

and the failure rate
has not come down at all.

So what happened when I started
my first business in water filtration?

The technology just wasn't there
for me to take any shortcuts.

I had no choice but to put in the work
and speak to lots of potential clients.
So I deeply understood what they needed.

But in my second business,
I barely spoke to any customers.

I had a solution and I was searching
for a problem like so many.

I used the technology crutch
to jump straight

into something that looked like
a business, but didn't have any substance.

I realized that we shifted
the challenge from lack of resources
to lack of reason. What do I mean by that?

Entrepreneurship used to take time,
money, technical know how.
Now it's as easy as sending a text.

So you get a website, you build
an email list, you create an app.

You get armies of drones to fly to all
your customers at the press of a button.

But you struggle because your business
lacks a reason to exist,

and you didn't do the work
to figure out if it should.
When Airbnb got started,

they had no idea if anyone would be
willing to stay at a stranger's home
in order to test this.

They hosted people
on air mattresses in their flats.

When DoorDash started,
instead of jumping to fancy technology,

Tony Shu personally spoke to customers
and restaurant owners
and delivered the meals himself.

That's how he first learned the challenges
he would need to solve.

Aspiring business owners are getting
distracted by all the shiny

things that make it look
like they have a business,

but they aren't putting
in any of the hard work first.

They are faking it before they make it,
and it's never been easier to fake it.

I used to think that all
the hardship that I went through

in my first business was terrible,
and that it should have been easier,
but now I realize that it can't be easy.

If you never had to answer tough
questions and go through tough times,

then what's stopping anyone else
to getting to where you are?
It will be difficult.

It must be. Now. I don't know exactly
what that difficulty will look like
for you. Maybe it's sleepless nights.
Maybe it's facing the fear of rejection.
Maybe it's having to go much deeper

to figuring out if anyone
even needs what you're selling.
I know it won't be easy,

but once you create a business
that's worth existing.

You can use all the incredible power
of AI to help you grow,

which is what I've done
in my first business,
which is still going strong and

which is what I'm doing
with all the new ideas I've had.

So before you worry about all the amazing
things AI and tech do for you,
try this.
Actually do the thing and get paid for it.
Airbnb hosted people in their flats.
DoorDash manually delivered orders.
Get one happy customer, then get more.
I wish I could tell you it was easier,

but as a 17 year old arriving in America
dreaming of starting a business,

I already knew. If you
make something that matters,

you will never have to fake
it again. Thank you.
